questions.question: Define the Big O notation used to describe upper bounds of algorithms. Provide an example of a function f(n) and a simple function g(n) such that f(n) = O(g(n)).
keyPoints: Big O describes an upper bound: f(n) ≤ c·g(n) for n ≥ n0.
Constants and lower-order terms are ignored in the asymptotic comparison.
Explain the role of the constant c and the threshold n0.

questions.question: Explain the difference between Big Omega (Ω) and Big Theta (Θ) notations. Give one example for each that illustrates a tight bound.
keyPoints: Big Ω provides a lower bound: f(n) ≥ c·g(n) for n ≥ n0.
Big Θ provides both an upper and a lower bound: c1·g(n) ≤ f(n) ≤ c2·g(n).
Example Ω: n^3 = Ω(n^2); example Θ: 5n^2+3n = Θ(n^2).
Emphasize that Θ gives a tight bound while Ω may be loose.

questions.question: Given two functions f(n)=3n^2+2n+1 and g(n)=n^3, determine the asymptotic relationship between f(n) and g(n) using Big O, Big Ω, and Big Θ. Also, discuss the space complexity of an algorithm that allocates a two‑dimensional array of size n×n and explain how you would express it using asymptotic notation.
keyPoints: Compare growth rates: n^3 grows faster than n^2 → f(n)=O(g(n)), g(n)=Ω(f(n)), f(n)≠Θ(g(n)).
Space complexity of an n×n array is Θ(n^2) additional memory.
Space includes the array itself, not the input; explain how to choose the dominating term.
Show how to find constants c and n0 for the O and Ω proofs.

questions.question: State and prove the transitive property of Big O notation. Additionally, define the little‑o and little‑omega notations, give an example for each, and illustrate how they differ from their Big counterparts. Also, list the most common time‑complexity classes (constant, logarithmic, linear, linear‑logarithmic, quadratic, exponential) and provide a brief real‑world example of an algorithm for each class.
keyPoints: Transitive property: if f=O(g) and g=O(h) then f=O(h); proof uses definition with constants.
Little‑o means strictly smaller order: f(n)=o(g(n)) ⇒ lim_{n→∞} f(n)/g(n)=0; example: n=o(n^2).
Little‑omega is the opposite: f(n)=ω(g(n)) ⇒ lim f/g = ∞; example: n^2=ω(n).
Common classes: O(1) – hash table lookup; O(log n) – binary search; O(n) – linear search; O(n log n) – merge sort; O(n^2) – bubble sort; O(2^n) – recursive subset generation.
